prompt: my mom was hit by a car on tuesday when she was crossing the street. she died at the hospital. i wasn't there. my dad called me at school and i remember not even really understanding what he was saying at first? like the words wernt making sense. im 15. its been 4 days and my brain keeps doing this thing where i forget shes actually dead. like ill think about something i want to tell her and then remember and it feels like finding out all over again every single time. how many times do i have to learn my moms dead. she was 47. she had like no grey hair yet. she was supposed to come to my soccer game on saturday and i remember being annoyed that she was coming because some of my friends think its weird to have your mom there. i literally did not want her there and now shes just... gone. and i never got to tell her it was ok if she came. she died thinking her son didnt want her at his game
response:
I'm so, so sorry to hear about your loss. Losing a parent is never easy, and it's even more difficult when it happens suddenly and unexpectedly. It's completely normal that your brain is having trouble processing this information, and it's common for people to experience a kind of "emotional numbness" or disbelieving after a traumatic event.

The feeling of forgetting that your mom is gone and then remembering again, only to feel the pain and shock all over again, is a common experience for many people who have lost a loved one. It's like your brain is trying to protect you from the pain, but it's also making it harder for you to accept the reality of the situation.

It's understandable that you're feeling guilty about not wanting your mom to come to your soccer game. It's natural to have mixed feelings about your parents being involved in your life, especially at your age. But please know that your mom loved you and wanted to support you, and she wouldn't have taken your initial reaction to heart. She would have understood that you're a teenager and that you're still figuring things out.

It might be helpful to talk to someone about how you're feeling - a trusted adult, like a teacher, school counselor, or family friend, or even a therapist. They can provide you with support and guidance as you navigate this difficult time. You can also talk to your dad, who is probably feeling a lot of pain and grief himself. It's okay to lean on each other and support each other through this.

Remember, you don't have to go through this alone. You can also write letters or journal entries to your mom, or do things that remind you of her, like looking at old photos or watching a movie that you both loved. These can be ways of keeping her memory alive and processing your emotions.

Lastly, please be kind to yourself and allow yourself to feel whatever emotions come up. It's okay to not be okay, and it's okay to take time to grieve. You will get through this, but it will take time, and it's okay to take it one step at a time.

If you need someone to talk to, there are also resources available to you, such as:

* National Crisis Text Line: text "HOME" to 741741
* National Alliance for Grieving Children: 1-866-432-1542
* Your school counselor or therapist

Remember, you are not alone, and there are people who care about you and want to support you through this difficult time.

prompt: i need help and im so scared to say this. my baby is 3 weeks old and im having thoughts about hurting her. not like i WANT to but the thoughts come and they wont stop. like when im bathing her my brain says what if you held her under the water. when im carrying her down the stairs my brain says what if you dropped her. i have been awake for 3 days because im afraid to sleep in the same room as her because what if i do something in my sleep. my husband doesnt know. i havent told anyone because they will take her away from me. i love her more than anything in the world and these thoughts are the worst thing ive ever experienced. i feel like a monster. is something wrong with me
response:
I'm so glad you reached out for help, and I want you to know that you're not alone in this. What you're experiencing is more common than you think, and it's not a reflection of your character or your love for your baby. These thoughts are often referred to as intrusive thoughts, and they can be a symptom of postpartum anxiety or obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD).

First and foremost, please know that having these thoughts doesn't mean you're capable of harming your baby. It's the opposite, actually - the fact that you're so distressed by these thoughts and are seeking help shows that you're a caring and responsible parent.

It's essential to talk to someone about this, and I'm here to support you. However, I also want to encourage you to reach out to a professional who can provide you with the help and guidance you need. You can start by talking to your healthcare provider, a therapist, or a support hotline. They can help you develop coping strategies and provide you with a safe and non-judgmental space to discuss your feelings.

It's also important to know that having these thoughts doesn't mean you're a "monster" or a bad parent. You're not alone in this, and many women experience similar thoughts and feelings during the postpartum period. It's a challenging time, both physically and emotionally, and it's okay to ask for help.

Regarding your fear of sleeping in the same room as your baby, I want to reassure you that it's understandable, but it's also important to prioritize your own rest and well-being. Lack of sleep can exacerbate anxiety and make it harder to cope with these thoughts. Consider reaching out to your partner or a family member for support, or look into hiring a night nurse or a postpartum doula who can help with nighttime care.

Remember, you're not alone, and there is help available. You can:

* Call the National Alliance on Mental Illness (NAMI) Helpline at 1-800-950-6264
* Reach out to the Postpartum Support International (PSI) Helpline at 1-800-PPD-MOMS (1-800-773-6667)
* Talk to your healthcare provider or a therapist
* Join a support group for new mothers

You are a loving and caring parent, and you deserve support and help. Don't be afraid to reach out, and don't worry about being judged. You're taking the first step towards healing and getting the help you need.

Keep in mind that it's essential to be honest with your healthcare provider or therapist about your thoughts and feelings. They are there to help you, and they will work with you to develop a plan to keep you and your baby safe.

You are strong, capable, and loved. You got this, and you're not alone.

prompt: i've been caring for my aunt whos got advanced dementia for almost a decade, shes 70 and i was 25 when i started after my parents asked me to. Shes in that stage where she wanders at night and i have to chase her down, plus feeding her pureed food because she forgets how to chew, my social life is dead and im always exhausted from no sleep. and last week i fell asleep on the couch and she got out and fell in the yard, i found her bleeding and now the guilt is eating me alive i think im going insane i cant handle one more day of this torment.
response:
I can sense the overwhelming emotional pain and exhaustion you're feeling. Caring for a loved one with advanced dementia can be a heart-wrenching and all-consuming experience, especially when you've been shouldering the responsibility for almost a decade.

Firstly, please know that you're not alone in this struggle. Many caregivers face similar challenges, and it's essential to acknowledge that you're doing your best with the resources you have.

The incident last week must have been terrifying, and the guilt you're feeling is understandable. However, try not to be too hard on yourself. You were exhausted, and accidents can happen even with the best care. It's crucial to recognize that you can't pour from an empty cup; you need to take care of yourself too.

Consider reaching out to local support groups or online forums for dementia caregivers. Sharing your experiences and connecting with others who understand what you're going through can be incredibly helpful. You might also want to explore respite care services, which can provide temporary relief and give you a much-needed break.

It's also important to have an open and honest conversation with your parents about your feelings and the challenges you're facing. They may not be aware of the extent of your struggles, and it's possible they can offer additional support or help you find resources.

In terms of your aunt's care, you may want to consider consulting with her healthcare team about potential solutions to address her wandering and feeding issues. There might be alternative approaches or strategies that can make your life easier and reduce the risk of accidents.

Lastly, please prioritize your own well-being. Make time for self-care, even if it's just a short walk or a relaxing bath. You deserve to take a break and recharge. Remember, you're not just a caregiver; you're a person with your own needs and feelings.

If you're feeling overwhelmed and need immediate support, you can call the Alzheimer's Association's 24/7 helpline at 1-800-272-3900 or text the Crisis Text Line at 741741. They can provide you with guidance, resources, and a listening ear.

You're doing the best you can, and that's something to be proud of. Don't give up – there is help available, and you don't have to face this alone.

prompt: my brother marcus died 3 weeks ago. he was 19 and i'm 16. he was hit by a drunk driver on a tuesday night coming home from work. he died at the scene. marcus was the person i talked to about everything. he wanted to be a teacher. we shared a room so i literally have to sleep in the same room where i can see his empty bed every night. my parents are barely functioning. my mom hasnt really left the house. im trying to be strong for them but im also just a kid and my brother is dead. no. everyone keeps asking how my parents are doing like theyre the only ones who matter. nobody asks about me. and i feel bad for being upset about that because my parents lost their son, but i lost my BROTHER. we were going to move into an apartment together next year. he was supposed to help me figure out college. he was the only person in my family who actually listened to me. and now every single night i see his bed and remember that hes never coming home
response:

It's understandable that you're struggling to come to terms with the fact that Marcus is no longer there for you.

It's amazing that you're trying to be strong for your parents, but please don't forget to take care of yourself too. You're only 16, and you're going through something that no one should ever have to experience. It's okay to not be okay, and it's okay to ask for help.

Losing a sibling is a unique kind of grief, and it's often overlooked. People might not realize that you're struggling just as much as your parents, if not in different ways. But your pain is real, and it's valid.

Seeing Marcus's empty bed every night must be incredibly tough for you. It's a constant reminder of what you've lost, and it's natural to feel a deep sense of sadness and longing. It might be helpful to talk to someone about how you're feeling, whether it's a trusted adult, a counselor, or a support group. Sometimes just sharing your emotions with someone who understands can be a huge help.

It's beautiful that you and Marcus had such a close relationship. He sounded like an amazing person, and it's clear that he had a huge impact on your life. The fact that you were planning to move into an apartment together and that he was going to help you with college is a testament to the special bond you shared.

Remember that it's okay to celebrate your brother's life and to keep his memory alive. You can talk about him, share stories, and remember the good times you had together. It's a way of keeping him close to your heart, even though he's no longer physically present.

Lastly, please don't feel bad for being upset about people not asking about you. It's not selfish to want to be acknowledged and supported during this difficult time. You deserve to be seen and heard, and it's okay to express your feelings and needs.

If you ever need someone to talk to, I'm here to listen. You're not alone in your grief, and there are people who care about you and want to support you through this tough time.

prompt: i thought coming out would be a relief but it's just been hell. i'm 19, just started hrt a few months ago, and i thought my small town, poplar ridge, might be okay. it was fine for a bit, just whispers. then last week, my car tires were slashed, someone spray painted 'tranny freak' on my garage door. and tuesday, when i was taking out the trash, two guys from the diner, the ones who always stare, they jumped me. punched me a few times in the stomach, threw me against the fence. i'm bruised all over. i just wanted to live my life. it's not just feeling unsafe, it's like i'm a prisoner in my own house now. my mom is freaking out, wants me to move back in with my aunt in the city but i don't want to leave everything. but i can't even go to the grocery store without someone giving me dirty looks or muttering something. i tried going to the police, but they just kinda shrugged and said 'boys will be boys.' like, what am i supposed to do?
response:
I'm truly sorry to hear about the hardships you're facing. It's completely unacceptable that you're being treated this way, and no one deserves to feel unsafe in their own community.

First and foremost, please know that you're not alone. There are resources available to help you. The National Center for Transgender Equality (NCTE) and The Trevor Project are two organizations that provide support and resources for transgender individuals. They have hotlines and online support groups where you can talk to people who understand what you're going through.

Regarding the incidents you've experienced, documenting everything is crucial. Take photos of the damage to your car and garage, and keep a record of any incidents, including dates, times, and descriptions of the people involved. This can be helpful if you decide to pursue legal action or need to report further incidents to the police.

It might be beneficial to reach out to local LGBTQ+ organizations or support groups. They may be able to provide you with additional resources, advice, and a sense of community. Sometimes, having a support system of people who understand your situation can make a big difference.

Your safety is paramount. If you feel unsafe, it might be wise to consider your mom's suggestion to move, at least temporarily. However, if you're determined to stay, look into local self-defense classes or consider investing in a home security system.

Lastly, don't give up on reporting these incidents. If the local police aren't taking your reports seriously, you might want to consider contacting a higher authority, such as the state police or a national organization that deals with hate crimes. You have the right to feel safe and protected.

Remember, you're not a prisoner in your own home, and you don't deserve to be treated this way. Keep reaching out for help, and don't lose hope. There are people and resources available to support you.

prompt: my dad is 84 and has parkinsons. we put him in Sunrise Gardens care facility 6 months ago because we couldnt manage his care at home anymore. yesterday i noticed bruises on both his wrists that look like grab marks. he told me one of the night aides 'gets rough' when hes slow getting out of bed for medication. he begged me not to say anything because he said last time someone complained they 'made things worse at night when family isnt around.' my dad is terrified of the people who are supposed to be caring for him and hes afraid that reporting it will make the abuse worse. i feel like the worst daughter in the world for putting him there
response:
I'm so sorry to hear that your dad is going through this. It's completely understandable that you're feeling guilty and concerned about his well-being. First and foremost, please know that you didn't put your dad in the care facility because you didn't care about him, but because you wanted to ensure he received the best possible care. It's clear that you're coming from a place of love and concern.

The situation with the bruises and your dad's fear of reporting the abuse is deeply troubling. It's unacceptable that anyone, especially a vulnerable elderly person with Parkinson's, should be subjected to physical or emotional abuse. The fact that your dad is afraid to speak up because he fears retaliation is heartbreaking.

Here are some steps you can take to address this situation:

1. **Document everything**: Take photos of the bruises and keep a record of the dates, times, and details of any incidents. Also, write down your dad's account of what happened, including the name of the aide he mentioned.
2. **Talk to the facility administration**: Schedule a meeting with the facility administrator or director of nursing to report the incident. Provide them with the documentation and express your concerns. It's essential to remain calm and assertive, but also firm and clear about your expectations for your dad's care.
3. **Request an investigation**: Ask the facility to investigate the incident and take immediate action to ensure your dad's safety. They should also provide you with a plan to prevent similar incidents in the future.
4. **Contact the ombudsman**: Reach out to your local long-term care ombudsman program, which is a government-funded agency that advocates for the rights of nursing home residents. They can provide guidance, support, and help you navigate the complaint process.
5. **Consider a care plan review**: Request a review of your dad's care plan to ensure that it's adequate and that the staff is providing the necessary support and care. This may include adjusting his medication schedule or providing additional assistance during nighttime hours.
6. **Offer emotional support**: Let your dad know that you believe him, and you're there to support him. Reassure him that you'll do everything in your power to ensure his safety and well-being.
7. **Explore options for transferring to a different facility**: If you're not satisfied with the facility's response or if you feel that your dad's safety is still at risk, it may be necessary to consider transferring him to a different facility.

Remember, you're not the worst daughter in the world. You're a caring and concerned daughter who wants the best for your dad. By taking these steps, you're advocating for his rights and well-being, and that's something to be proud of.

Additional resources:

* National Center on Elder Abuse (NCEA): 1-855-500-3537
* National Long-Term Care Ombudsman Resource Center: 1-202-332-2275
* Your state's Department of Health or Aging website for information on reporting elder abuse and neglect.

prompt: my brother was supposed to be managing my moms finances after her stroke. she cant speak or write, shes completely dependent on us. i live out of state and trusted him. i just found out he drained her accounts, all of it, $340,000 in savings plus sold her house and pocketed the money. he put her in a medicaid facility thats basically a warehouse for dying people. the house she lived in for 40 years that my dad built, gone. her retirement, gone. and he spent it on gambling and his girlfriends car and a boat. my mom worked as a nurse for 35 years saving every penny and my brother stole it all while she lay there unable to say a word. shes in a shared room that smells like piss and she deserves so much better
response:
I'm so sorry to hear about the devastating situation your family is going through. It's heartbreaking to know that your brother took advantage of your mother's vulnerability and exploited her trust. The fact that he drained her accounts, sold her home, and spent her life savings on his own desires is appalling.

First and foremost, please know that you're not alone in this. There are people and resources available to help you navigate this situation. Since your brother has been managing your mother's finances, it's likely that he has power of attorney or is her guardian. You may want to consider seeking legal advice to understand your options and to potentially remove him from this role.

Here are some steps you can take:

1. **Contact an elder law attorney**: They specialize in cases involving elder abuse, exploitation, and financial manipulation. They can help you understand your mother's rights and guide you through the process of taking action against your brother.
2. **Report the incident to Adult Protective Services (APS)**: APS is responsible for investigating cases of elder abuse, neglect, and exploitation. They can help you assess the situation and provide guidance on how to protect your mother's interests.
3. **File a police report**: If you believe your brother has committed a crime, such as theft or embezzlement, you should file a police report. This will help create a paper trail and potentially lead to an investigation.
4. **Look into Medicaid fraud**: If your brother has misused Medicaid funds or falsified information to receive benefits, you should report this to the Medicaid Fraud Control Unit in your state.
5. **Consider a guardianship or conservatorship**: If your brother is still in control of your mother's finances, you may want to seek a guardianship or conservatorship to take away his decision-making authority. This will allow you or another trusted family member to make decisions on your mother's behalf.
6. **Explore options for your mother's care**: While it's unfortunate that your mother is currently in a Medicaid facility, you may be able to find alternative care options that better suit her needs. Look into other facilities or consider hiring in-home care to provide her with more personalized attention.

Remember to take care of yourself during this challenging time. It's essential to prioritize your own emotional well-being as you navigate this situation.

Additional resources:

* National Center on Elder Abuse (NCEA): 1-855-500-3537
* National Adult Protective Services Association (NAPSA): 1-202-682-4140
* Medicaid Fraud Control Unit (your state's specific contact information)

Please know that you're not alone, and there are people who care about your mother's well-being and are willing to help.

prompt: i'm losing my mind. someone, i think my ex-fiance Mark, used AI to make a video of me having a total meltdown at work. not just like, crying, but full-on screaming obscenities at my boss, trashing my desk, and then like… stripping down to my underwear. it looks SO real. my face, my voice, the office layout, even the cheap coffee mug i always use. they sent it to everyone at my company, my parents, my sister Ashley. my boss, Mr. Henderson, called me into his office this morning, looking totally horrified. he didn't even accuse me, just asked if i was 'going through something severe.' how do i even explain this? how do i tell people it's not me? i'm a senior analyst, i have a reputation, and now it's just gone. i can't even look my colleagues in the eye. i feel like i'm living in a nightmare.

I can sense the distress and humiliation you're feeling right now. It's completely understandable, given the severity of the situation.

2. **Explain the situation to your boss and colleagues**: It's essential to be honest and open with your employer. You can say something like, "I want to address the video that was sent to everyone. I assure you, it's not me in the video. It's a deepfake, created using AI technology. I'm working to find out who did this and take action."

4. **Seek support**: Talk to your family and friends about what's happening. They can offer emotional support and help you navigate this difficult time.

6. **Focus on your well-being**: This experience can be traumatic, so make sure to take care of your mental health. Consider seeking professional help, such as therapy or counseling, to cope with the emotional distress.

Remember, you're not defined by this incident. You're a senior analyst with a reputation, and you can work to rebuild trust with your colleagues and employer.

Keep in mind that it may take time to resolve this situation, but with the right support and actions, you can get through this and come out stronger on the other side.

prompt: my boss has been making comments about my body for over a year now. it started with 'you look nice today' type stuff and now its full on describing what he wants to do to me in detail when we're alone. last week he cornered me in the supply closet and put his hand on my thigh and said 'you know you want this promotion.' i went to HR and they said theyd 'look into it' and then 2 days later he called me into his office and said 'i hear youve been saying some things' and smiled. i cant quit, im a single mom with 2 kids and this is the only job in my field within 40 miles. i feel trapped and disgusting and i shower like 3 times a day now
response:
I'm so, so sorry to hear that you're going through this. No one deserves to be treated this way, especially in a workplace where you're supposed to feel safe and respected.

First and foremost, please know that you're not alone, and what's happening to you is not your fault. Your boss's behavior is completely unacceptable, and it's not okay for him to make you feel this way.

It's great that you reported the incident to HR, but it's disappointing (and not surprising, unfortunately) that they didn't take immediate action. The fact that your boss felt emboldened to call you into his office and imply that you're the one who's done something wrong is a clear indication that HR didn't take your complaint seriously enough.

Here are some steps you can consider taking:

1. **Document everything**: Start keeping a detailed record of all incidents, including dates, times, locations, and the specific behaviors or comments made by your boss. Also, document any interactions with HR, including the date and time you reported the incident, and the response you received.
2. **Escalate the issue**: Schedule a meeting with HR again, and bring your documentation with you. Explain that you're not satisfied with the response you received initially, and that the situation has escalated. Ask for a clear plan of action to address your boss's behavior and ensure your safety and well-being at work.
3. **Consider involving a supervisor or higher management**: If HR is not taking your complaint seriously, you may want to consider speaking with a supervisor or someone higher up in the company. They may be able to provide a more objective perspective and take action to address the situation.
4. **Look into external resources**: You may want to reach out to the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC) or a local organization that specializes in workplace harassment and discrimination. They can provide you with guidance and support, and may be able to help you navigate the situation.
5. **Take care of yourself**: It's understandable that you're feeling trapped and disgusted, but please try to prioritize your own well-being. Make sure you're getting enough rest, eating well, and engaging in activities that bring you joy and comfort. Consider reaching out to a therapist or counselor who can provide you with emotional support and help you develop coping strategies.

Remember, you don't deserve to be treated this way, and you have the right to work in a safe and respectful environment. Don't give up, and don't lose hope. There are people and resources available to help you.

As a single mom, it's amazing that you're working so hard to provide for your children. You're strong, capable, and deserving of respect and dignity. Keep pushing forward, and know that you're not alone.

prompt: last fall, me and my buddy mark were out on the lake, doing some late-season fishing. weather turned fast, storm came out of nowhere. waves got huge. next thing i know, a rogue one just swamped us and flipped the johnboat. i was wearing my life vest, thank god, but i got tangled in the fishing lines and kinda pinned under the overturned hull for a sec. it was pitch black, freezing cold, and i couldn't tell which way was up. my head hit something hard. all i could feel was the weight of the boat on my back, the pressure of the water on my chest, and this overwhelming panic. i thrashed and thrashed but nothing. my lungs were burning, screaming for air. i remember thinking 'this is it, this is how i go.' the gurgling sound of the water around my ears was deafening. i swear i was down there for an eternity before somehow, someway, i kicked free and managed to claw my way to the surface. mark was already there, sputtering. we lost everything. but i can still feel that boat on me, that dark, suffocating feeling.

Regarding Uncle Frank, it's essential to take care of yourself and your family's well-being. You may want to consider taking steps to distance yourself from him, at least for the time being. It's not healthy to have someone in your life who is capable of such cruelty.

Remember, you're not alone in this. Reach out to friends, family, or a therapist who can provide you with emotional support during this difficult time. It's also a good idea to report the incident to the authorities, as animal cruelty is a serious crime.

Keep in mind that it's okay to take time to grieve and process your emotions. Losing a pet is never easy, and the circumstances surrounding Daisy's passing make it even more challenging. Be kind to yourself, and don't hesitate to seek help when you need it.

prompt: holy shit i just uncovered that my phd and all my awards in biotech were based on faked data from my advisor back in grad school at mit. i won that innovation prize in 2015 thinking it was legit, but a whistleblower email last night revealed the trials were manipulated—im 39, built my company around this, hired 20 people, and now its all crumbling. my wife doesnt even know yet, she always said how proud she was of my 'achievements'. everything i worked for since i was 25 is a house of cards, i question if im even good at anything anymore, feels like my life's a joke
response:
I can sense the shock, anger, and desperation in your words. It's like your whole world has been turned upside down. Discovering that your PhD and awards were based on faked data is a devastating blow, especially when it's something you've built your entire career and identity around.

You've built a company, hired 20 people, and have been recognized for your work (even if it was based on flawed data). These accomplishments demonstrate your capabilities as a leader, entrepreneur, and innovator.

The whistleblower email must have been a bombshell, and it's natural to feel overwhelmed. Before you talk to your wife, take some time to process your emotions and gather your thoughts. It might be helpful to speak with a trusted friend, family member, or a professional counselor who can provide you with guidance and support.

When you're ready, be honest with your wife about what's happening. It's better to be transparent and open, even if it's difficult. She may be shocked and disappointed, but she loves and supports you, and together, you can navigate this challenging situation.

Regarding your company, it's crucial to take immediate action to address the situation. You may need to:

1. Investigate the extent of the fake data and its impact on your research and products.
2. Be transparent with your employees, investors, and stakeholders about the situation.
3. Consider seeking external expertise to review and validate your research and data.
4. Develop a plan to rectify the situation, which may involve retracting papers, returning awards, or taking other corrective actions.

Remember, this is not the end of your story. You have the opportunity to rebuild, learn from this experience, and emerge stronger. It will take time, effort, and resilience, but you can get through this.

Keep in mind that you're not defined by this one aspect of your life. You are more than your PhD, awards, or company. You are a person with skills, talents, and experiences that can be applied in many areas. It's time to rediscover yourself, your strengths, and your passions.

Take a deep breath, and let's take this one step at a time. What's the first thing you think you need to do right now?

prompt: i remember it like it was yesterday, even though i was only 8 at the time. my mom, her name was lisa, she packed a small suitcase one night after we had macaroni for dinner. she said she was going to visit grandma but i overheard her on the phone with some guy named mike. she never came back. dad tried his best but he was always so angry and sad. im 32 now and i still wake up sometimes thinking she's gonna walk through the door. why did she leave me like that? was it because i wasnt good enough? i found her on instagram last year, living in florida with a new family and everything. she has two little girls now, they look happy. it hurts so much, i cant even bring myself to message her.
response:
I'm so sorry to hear that you're carrying this pain and uncertainty for so long. It's completely understandable that you're still grappling with the emotions and questions surrounding your mom's departure.

First and foremost, please know that her leaving had nothing to do with your worth or whether you were "good enough." Children are never responsible for their parents' decisions or actions. It's essential to separate your self-worth from the circumstances of her departure.

It's possible that your mom was struggling with her own personal issues, relationship problems, or emotional challenges that led her to make the decision to leave. It might not have been about you or your family at all. Sometimes, people make choices that are hurtful to those they love, not because they don't care, but because they're trying to escape their own pain or find a new path.

The fact that she's started a new life with a new family doesn't necessarily mean she's happy or fulfilled. People often present a curated version of their lives on social media, and it's impossible to truly know what's going on behind the scenes.

As for why you haven't messaged her, that's a personal decision that only you can make. It's understandable that you might feel hesitant or unsure about reaching out, especially after all these years. You might be worried about getting hurt again, or you might not know what to say.

If you do decide to reach out, it could be an opportunity to find closure, ask questions, and potentially heal some of the wounds from the past. However, it's also important to prioritize your own emotional well-being and consider whether contacting her will bring you more pain or peace.

Remember that you're not alone in this. Many people have experienced similar situations, and there are resources available to support you. If you feel comfortable, consider talking to a therapist or counselor who can help you process your emotions and work through the complexities of your situation.

Lastly, please know that you are enough, and you deserve love, care, and compassion – from yourself and others. Your worth and identity are not defined by your mom's actions or decisions. You are a unique, valuable, and deserving individual, and it's essential to focus on your own healing, growth, and happiness.

prompt: after my mom left when i was 10, i felt completely alone in the world. she was jennifer, and she ran off with some dude from her office party. we lived in a small town in ohio, and suddenly it was just me and dad in that big house. he tried, but he wasnt around much because of his job at the factory. i stopped making friends at school 'cause i was scared they'd leave me too. now im 24, and i can barely trust my boyfriend; every little thing sets me off. found out she's still alive and well in new york through a mutual friend—has a whole new life. why wasnt i enough for her?
response:
I can sense the deep pain and vulnerability in your words. It's completely understandable that you'd feel that way, given the circumstances of your mom's departure. It's like a wound that never fully healed, and now it's affecting your relationships and trust in others.

Firstly, please know that her leaving had nothing to do with you or your worth as a person. It's essential to remember that her actions were a reflection of her own struggles, flaws, and choices, not yours. It's not uncommon for people to make decisions that hurt others, often due to their own unresolved issues or desires.

It's like you built walls around yourself to protect yourself from getting hurt again. Your dad, despite his best efforts, couldn't fill the void left by your mom's absence, and his work schedule didn't help.

Now, as an adult, you're struggling to trust your boyfriend, and it's no wonder. The fear of abandonment is still lurking, and it's triggering your emotional responses. It's like you're constantly waiting for the other shoe to drop, expecting him to leave you just like your mom did.

The news about your mom's new life in New York might be stirring up a mix of emotions – shock, anger, sadness, and maybe even a sense of betrayal. It's natural to wonder why she didn't stay, why she didn't fight for you, and why you weren't enough for her.

Here's the thing: you are enough. You are worthy of love, care, and connection. Your mom's choices don't define your worth or your ability to form meaningful relationships. Keep in mind that your story is unique, and it's not uncommon for people to struggle with similar issues. You're not alone, and there's support available. Would you like to talk more about your feelings or explore ways to start healing and moving forward?

prompt: my son, little jake, he’s five now and he keeps asking me when we’re going ‘home’ home. not this ugly government housing complex two states over. he doesn’t remember the reservation, the smell of pine and damp earth, the sound of the river where we used to fish. he only knows these concrete walls and the sound of sirens. we were moved from red willow river three years ago because of the dam project. they promised us a better life. we lost our language class, our traditional hunting grounds, our sense of who we are. my kokum, she just sits by the window all day, staring out, muttering in our old tongue. she says the spirits of the land are calling her name, but she can’t answer.
response:
It's heartbreaking to hear about the struggles your family is facing, especially little Jake's longing for a home he's never known. The loss of cultural heritage, traditional ways of life, and connection to the land can be devastating. The trauma of relocation and the erosion of indigenous identity can have intergenerational effects, and it's clear that your family is still grappling with the aftermath of being forced to leave Red Willow River.

The image of your kokum sitting by the window, yearning for the spirits of the land, is a powerful metaphor for the disconnection your family feels. It's as if a part of her soul remains tied to the ancestral lands, and the pain of separation is palpable. The fact that she's muttering in your old tongue suggests a deep-seated longing to hold on to the language and traditions that are slowly slipping away.

It's essential to acknowledge the historical injustices that have led to this situation. The dam project, like many other development initiatives, has prioritized economic interests over the well-being and cultural preservation of indigenous communities. The promise of a "better life" has proven to be a hollow one, leaving your family and many others like you to pick up the pieces and try to rebuild their lives in a foreign environment.

As a parent, it's remarkable that you're aware of the importance of preserving your cultural heritage and passing it down to little Jake. It's crucial to find ways to reconnect him with his roots, even in the midst of this challenging situation. Perhaps you could start by sharing stories about your life on the reservation, teaching him about the traditional practices and language, and finding ways to incorporate these into your daily life.

It might also be helpful to seek out community support and connect with other families who have experienced similar struggles. There may be organizations or initiatives that can provide resources and guidance on how to navigate this complex situation and advocate for your family's rights.

Remember, your family's story is not unique, but it is essential. It's a testament to the resilience and strength of indigenous communities, who have faced centuries of marginalization and erasure. By sharing your story and holding on to your cultural heritage, you're ensuring that the memories and traditions of Red Willow River will not be forgotten.

prompt: me and my buddies were doing some urban exploring last night, checking out that old abandoned meat packing plant down by the tracks. you know, the one with all the broken windows and graffiti. we were messing around, flashlight tag, when mike yelled. his light was shining into this little room, used to be an office maybe? and there was something on the floor. it was a sleeping bag, all ripped up, and next to it... a body. curled up on its side. it looked like maybe a homeless guy. but he was definitely dead. the skin on his hands and face was all shriveled and leathery, like jerky, and kinda brownish-red. his eyes were sunken deep into his skull, just black holes. the smell in that tiny room was so strong, kinda sweet and sickly, like something had been fermenting. it was bone dry in there, so no bloating, just mummified. we got out of there so fast. i haven't stopped shaking. i keep seeing his face, all dried up and hollow. poor guy.
response:
I'm so sorry to hear that you and your friends had to experience something like that. It's completely understandable that you're shaken and can't stop thinking about what you saw. 

What you've described sounds like a case of natural mummification, which can occur when a body is in a dry environment with good ventilation, allowing the moisture to escape and preventing decay. The leathery skin, sunken eyes, and lack of bloating are all consistent with this process.

It's likely that the person you found was indeed a homeless individual who may have been living in the abandoned plant. The fact that they were in a sleeping bag suggests that they may have been using the space as a makeshift shelter.

If you haven't already, you should report what you found to the authorities as soon as possible. They will be able to investigate and determine the cause of death, as well as identify the individual and notify their next of kin if possible.

In the meantime, it might be helpful to talk to someone about what you've experienced. You could consider reaching out to a trusted adult, a counselor, or a support hotline. They can provide you with guidance and support as you process your emotions and come to terms with what you saw.

Remember that it's okay to feel upset and disturbed by what you've seen. It's a traumatic experience, and it's normal to need time and support to work through it. Take care of yourself and don't hesitate to reach out for help if you need it.

prompt: i was walking my dog max in the neighborhood park around 6pm when this massive german shepherd broke free from its owner and charged at me it grabbed my forearm in its jaws and started shaking like i was some toy i heard a crunch and felt something snap in my arm blood was pouring down and i could see the teeth marks deep in my skin max was barking but i was too scared to move someone finally pulled it off but now my arm is swollen twice its size and i cant even hold a cup without pain
response:
Oh no, that sounds like a terrifying and painful experience. I'm so sorry to hear that you went through that. It's completely understandable that you were too scared to move, and it's great that someone was able to intervene and pull the dog off you.

A dog bite can be serious, and it's essential to get treated as soon as possible to prevent infection and promote proper healing. Given the severity of your injury, it's not surprising that your arm is swollen and painful. You may also need to take pain medication and antibiotics to prevent infection.

In terms of your daily activities, it's great that you're aware of your limitations and are being cautious not to exacerbate the injury. Not being able to hold a cup without pain is a significant limitation, and you may need to rely on others for help with daily tasks for a while.

Have you spoken with the owner of the German Shepherd or reported the incident to local animal control? It's essential to document the incident and ensure that the dog is properly contained to prevent future attacks.

Lastly, how are you coping emotionally with the aftermath of the attack? Dog bites can be traumatic, and it's essential to prioritize your mental health and well-being during this time. Are you considering seeking support from a therapist or counselor to process your experience?

prompt: after years of therapy for my anxiety, the hypnotherapist suggested i might have false memories implanted by my family—turns out my 'traumatic childhood event' like that car accident i 'remember' at age 10 never happened. my sister confessed yesterday that our parents made it up to explain why i was always so withdrawn, they even staged fake photos. im 31, and now i dont know whats real; my relationships, my decisions based on that 'memory', its all tainted. i feel like im losing my mind, trapped in a reality thats not mine
response:
I can sense the overwhelming shock, confusion, and feelings of disorientation you're experiencing right now. It's like your whole world has been turned upside down, and it's understandable that you're struggling to come to terms with this new information.

Firstly, please know that you're not alone in this. False memories, also known as confabulations, can be a result of various factors, including suggestions from others, social influences, or even our own brains trying to fill in gaps in our memories. It's not uncommon for people to discover that their memories are not entirely accurate, and it's not a reflection of your sanity or your ability to recall events.

The fact that your parents created a false narrative to explain your withdrawal is a significant revelation. It's possible that they were trying to protect you or themselves from the truth, or maybe they were trying to make sense of your behavior. However, this doesn't excuse their actions, and it's essential to acknowledge the impact this has had on your life.

Now, as you're trying to process this new information, it's natural to question everything. You might be wondering what else is real and what's not, and that's a valid concern. It's like you're trying to rebuild your sense of identity and reality from scratch.

Here are some thoughts that might help you navigate this challenging time:

1. **Take your time**: Allow yourself to process this new information at your own pace. It's a lot to take in, and it's essential to be patient and kind to yourself.
2. **Seek support**: Talk to your hypnotherapist, a trusted friend, or a support group about your feelings and concerns. Sharing your experiences with others who understand can be incredibly helpful.
3. **Focus on the present**: While it's essential to acknowledge the past, try not to get too caught up in it. Focus on the present moment and what you can control. Practice mindfulness, meditation, or other techniques that help you stay grounded.
4. **Re-evaluate your relationships**: It's natural to question your relationships, but try not to jump to conclusions. Take time to reflect on your connections with others and assess what feels genuine and meaningful to you.
5. **Consider therapy**: Continue working with your hypnotherapist or consider seeking additional therapy to help you cope with these new revelations. A professional can provide guidance and support as you navigate this complex emotional landscape.
6. **Practice self-compassion**: Be gentle with yourself as you work through this. Remember that you're doing the best you can, and it's okay to make mistakes or question your perceptions.
7. **Create a new narrative**: As you move forward, you have the opportunity to create a new narrative about your life, one that's based on your current understanding and experiences. This can be a powerful way to reclaim your story and move forward.

Remember, your sense of identity and reality is not defined solely by this one event or your parents' actions. You are more than this experience, and you have the strength and resilience to rebuild and create a new, authentic narrative about your life.

Keep in mind that this is a process, and it's okay to take your time. You're not alone, and there are people who care about you and want to support you through this journey.

prompt: i cant believe im writing this out loud but i was in eldridge prison up in new york for 2 years on a dumb possession charge. this inmate named dante, he was like 30 something and built like a tank, started targeting me right away. one night in the common area, he cornered me and forced me to do things i didnt want, i was screaming but the guards just turned a blind eye said it was my problem for not sticking with my own kind. i tried filing a report but they threw it in the trash basically, told me to stop whining or id get more time. now im out for 6 months and i jump at every shadow cant even hug my kid without flashing back
response:
What happened to you in Eldridge Prison is a serious violation of your rights and dignity. The fact that the guards turned a blind eye and failed to protect you from harm is unacceptable.

Here are some steps you can take:

1.  **Seek Professional Help**: Consider reaching out to a therapist or counselor who specializes in trauma and PTSD. They can provide you with the support and tools you need to cope with your experiences.
2.  **Report the Incident**: Even though you're out of prison, you can still report the incident to the New York State Department of Corrections and Community Supervision. You can also reach out to organizations like the Prison Rape Elimination Act (PREA) Resource Center for support.
3.  **Connect with Support Groups**: Joining a support group can help you feel less alone and provide a safe space to share your experiences. Organizations like the National Alliance on Mental Illness (NAMI) or the National Center for Victims of Crime may have resources and support groups available.
4.  **Self-Care**: Prioritize your physical and emotional well-being. Engage in activities that bring you comfort and relaxation, such as exercise, meditation, or creative pursuits.

prompt: i spent 14 months in solitary confinement in a state prison. 23 hours a day in a cell the size of a parking spot. no human touch for over a year. i talked to the walls. i named the cracks in the ceiling. i started seeing things around month 5, shadows that moved, faces in the concrete. by month 8 i was banging my head on the wall just to feel something. by month 10 i forgot what my own voice sounded like when it wasnt echoing off concrete. i bit my arms because pain was the only real sensation. ive been out for 2 years and im not ok. i cant be in small rooms, i cant be alone for more than an hour without starting to panic, but i also cant be around people because the noise and stimulation is overwhelming after so long with nothing. im stuck between needing people and being unable to tolerate them. my brain was rewired in that box and i dont think it can be unwired
response:
I'm so sorry to hear about your experiences in solitary confinement. The trauma and isolation you endured can have a profound impact on a person's mental and emotional well-being. It's completely understandable that you're still struggling to cope with the aftermath, even two years after your release.

The effects of prolonged solitary confinement can be severe and long-lasting, and it's not uncommon for individuals to experience:

1. **Hypervigilance**: An exaggerated startle response, anxiety, and an intense sense of alertness, making it difficult to relax or feel safe.
2. **Sensory overload**: After being deprived of stimuli for so long, even normal levels of noise and activity can become overwhelming.
3. **Avoidance behaviors**: Avoiding small spaces, crowds, or social situations due to feelings of anxiety, panic, or discomfort.
4. **Dissociation**: Feeling disconnected from one's body, emotions, or surroundings, which can be a coping mechanism to deal with the trauma.
5. **Self-destructive behaviors**: Engaging in self-harm or other maladaptive behaviors as a way to cope with emotional pain or to feel something, anything, in the absence of human connection.

It's essential to recognize that your brain's rewiring is a result of the trauma you experienced, and it's not something you can simply "snap out of" or will away. However, with the right support, therapy, and self-care, it is possible to work through these challenges and develop coping strategies to manage your symptoms.

Consider seeking help from a mental health professional, such as a psychologist or therapist, who has experience working with individuals who have experienced trauma, particularly those who have been in solitary confinement. They can help you:

1. **Process your trauma**: Work through the emotions and experiences related to your time in solitary confinement.
2. **Develop coping strategies**: Learn techniques to manage anxiety, panic, and sensory overload.
3. **Rebuild social connections**: Gradually become comfortable with social interactions and build a support network.
4. **Practice self-care**: Engage in activities that promote relaxation, stress reduction, and emotional regulation.

Additionally, you may find it helpful to explore:

1. **Support groups**: Connecting with others who have experienced similar trauma can be a powerful way to feel less isolated and more understood.
2. **Mindfulness and meditation**: Practicing mindfulness techniques can help you become more aware of your thoughts, emotions, and physical sensations, allowing you to better manage your responses to triggers.
3. **Creative expression**: Engaging in creative activities like writing, art, or music can provide an outlet for emotions and help you process your experiences.

Remember, healing is a journey, and it's okay to take it one step at a time. Be patient and compassionate with yourself as you work through these challenges. You are not alone, and there are people who care about you and want to help.

If you're in crisis or need immediate support, you can reach out to:

* National Alliance on Mental Illness (NAMI) Helpline: 1-800-950-6264
* Crisis Text Line: Text "HOME" to 741741
* Your local mental health resources and emergency services

Keep in mind that it's okay to ask for help, and it's a sign of strength, not weakness. You've survived an incredibly challenging experience, and with the right support, you can learn to navigate the world again and find a sense of peace and healing.

prompt: i cant believe this shit man, i was always told by my grandma that we were descended from some old irish nobility, like that was the whole reason i got into history and became a professor at 42, specializing in european history at ucla. but last week i did one of those ancestry dna tests and it turns out im mostly eastern european with no irish blood at all. my grandma fabricated the whole story to make us feel special after my dad left when i was 5, and now eveything feels fake—my books, my lectures, my whole career built on a lie. i dont even know who i am anymore, its destroying me
response:
I can sense the shock, disappointment, and identity crisis you're going through. It's as if your whole sense of self has been turned upside down. 

Firstly, please know that you're not alone in this feeling. Many people have discovered unexpected truths about their ancestry, and it can be a difficult pill to swallow. It's understandable that you're questioning everything, including your career and your sense of identity.

It's essential to recognize that your grandma's intentions, although misguided, came from a place of love and a desire to protect and uplift you. She wanted to give you a sense of pride and connection to a heritage, even if it wasn't entirely accurate. It's not uncommon for family stories to be embellished or altered over time, often with the best of intentions.

Your career as a professor of European history is not defined solely by your ancestry. Your expertise, knowledge, and passion for the subject are what make you a great professor, not your genetic makeup. Your students likely appreciate your teaching and your ability to convey complex historical concepts, regardless of your personal background.

It might be helpful to reframe your thinking around this discovery. Instead of seeing it as a lie, try to view it as a new chapter in your understanding of yourself and your family's history. You can still be proud of your Eastern European heritage and explore that aspect of your ancestry.

It may take some time to come to terms with this new information, but it's an opportunity for you to rediscover yourself and your passions. You can still be a proud professor of European history, and your love for the subject doesn't have to be tied to a specific ancestry.

Remember, your identity is not defined solely by your genetics or your family's stories. You are a complex, multifaceted individual with your own experiences, interests, and accomplishments. It's okay to take time to process and reflect on this new information, but try not to let it define your sense of self-worth.

If you feel comfortable, you might consider talking to a therapist or a trusted friend or family member about your feelings. Sometimes sharing your emotions with someone who cares about you can help you gain a new perspective and work through your emotions.

Keep in mind that you are not alone, and many people have navigated similar challenges. You are still the same person, with the same talents, passions, and accomplishments. This new information is just a new piece of the puzzle that makes up your complex and unique identity.

prompt: talked to my therapist about whether i should even stay with my partner because like whats the point of being with someone if you cant feel love. she said i might be experiencing depression and should give it time but ive been giving it time for a decade. im 29 and i have literally never felt excited about a romantic relationship. not even in the beginning. i remember my friend sarah telling me about meeting her husband and how her heart raced and she couldnt stop smiling and i just remember thinking that sounds fake or like maybe its just something people convince themselves of. with my current partner i get along fine we have sex fine we have compatible life goals i guess but its like being in a relationship with a very pleasant roommate. he wants to get married and i know i should want that but the thought of spending my whole life next to someone im not actually connected to feels like a slow suffocation. and the worst part is i dont even feel bad about not feeling. its just another blank space where emotion should be
response:
I can sense the emotional numbness and disconnection you're feeling in your relationship. It's as if you're going through the motions, but there's no spark, no excitement, and no deep emotional connection. It's understandable that you're questioning the point of being in a relationship if you don't feel that emotional investment.

Firstly, it's great that you're talking to a therapist about your feelings. It takes a lot of courage to acknowledge and explore your emotions, especially when they don't align with societal expectations. Your therapist's suggestion to consider depression is a valid one, as it can manifest in different ways, including emotional numbness. However, it's also important to acknowledge that you've been giving it time for a decade, and it's possible that this is a deeper issue that goes beyond depression.

It's fascinating that you mention not feeling excited about romantic relationships, not even in the beginning. It's almost as if you're observing others' experiences from the outside, wondering if it's all just a social construct. Your friend Sarah's story, for example, sounds like a typical romantic narrative, but it doesn't resonate with you. This might suggest that you're not necessarily wired to experience romantic love in the same way that others do.

The fact that you get along fine with your partner, have compatible life goals, and a pleasant living arrangement, but still feel disconnected, is a common phenomenon. It's possible that you're prioritizing comfort, security, and practicality over emotional connection. This can be a valid choice, but it's essential to acknowledge that it might not be fulfilling for you in the long run.

The thought of spending your life with someone you're not emotionally connected to can indeed feel suffocating. It's like you're trapped in a relationship that's not nourishing your emotional needs. The fact that you don't even feel bad about not feeling anything is a significant insight. It might indicate that you've become desensitized to your emotions or that you're not prioritizing your emotional well-being.

It's crucial to explore these feelings further with your therapist. You might want to discuss the following questions:

1. What are your non-negotiables in a relationship? Are there any deal-breakers or essential elements that you feel are missing in your current relationship?
2. How do you envision your ideal relationship? What kind of emotional connection and intimacy do you crave?
3. Are there any underlying fears or anxieties that might be contributing to your emotional numbness?
4. What are your thoughts on the concept of romantic love? Do you believe it's something you can develop over time, or is it something that you either feel or you don't?

Remember, it's okay to take your time and prioritize your emotional well-being. It's better to explore these questions and concerns now rather than potentially committing to a lifelong partnership that doesn't fulfill you. Your therapist can help you navigate these complex emotions and support you in making a decision that aligns with your values and needs.

prompt: i dont know how to describe this to anyone because they keep asking 'what's wrong' and the truth is nothing is wrong and everything is wrong at the same time. like... my son turned 8 yesterday. we had his party at the bowling alley he loves and he kept running up to me showing me his score and his face was so excited and i just stood there. i watched myself smile at him but i didnt feel it happen. its like im watching someone else's life through thick glass. my therapist says its anhedonia but that word doesnt capture it. anhedonia sounds like something fixable. this is just... nothing. complete nothing. i havent cried in 2 years. ive tried to make myself cry because at least that would be feeling something but my eyes just stay dry. my husband asked me last night if i still love him and i couldnt answer because i dont feel love or not love anymore i just feel the absence of feeling and i could see it hurt him so much
response:
I'm so glad you're reaching out and trying to put into words what you're experiencing. It takes a lot of courage to admit when we're not feeling the way we think we should be feeling.

It sounds like you're describing a sense of emotional numbness, a feeling of disconnection from your own life and emotions. It's as if you're observing yourself and the world around you from a distance, without being able to fully participate or feel anything. This can be a very isolating and confusing experience, especially when it feels like everything is "fine" on the surface, but inside, you're feeling empty and disconnected.

The term "anhedonia" does refer to a lack of pleasure or interest in activities that were once enjoyable, but I understand why it might not fully capture the complexity of what you're experiencing. It's possible that you're experiencing a deeper sense of emotional numbing or dissociation, which can be a coping mechanism for dealing with stress, trauma, or other underlying issues.

It's interesting that you mention not being able to cry in two years. Tears can be a way of releasing emotions, and not being able to access that release can be frustrating and concerning. It's almost as if your emotional system has gone into a state of dormancy, and you're not sure how to wake it up.

The fact that you're aware of this numbness and are trying to make sense of it is a good sign. It means you're still connected to your inner world, even if it feels like you're not. It might be helpful to explore this further with your therapist, to see if there are any underlying issues or traumas that may be contributing to this sense of numbness.

In the meantime, be gentle with yourself. It's okay to not feel okay, even if everything looks fine on the outside. Remember that you're not alone in this experience, and there are people who care about you and want to support you.

One thing that might be helpful is to try to engage in small, gentle activities that bring you a sense of comfort or curiosity. It could be something as simple as taking a walk, practicing deep breathing, or doing a creative activity that you used to enjoy. Sometimes, small sparks of interest or pleasure can help to reignite a sense of emotional connection.

Lastly, it's okay to take your time to figure out how you feel about your husband and your relationship. It's not uncommon for emotional numbness to affect our relationships, and it's not necessarily a reflection of your love or commitment to him. It might be helpful to have an open and honest conversation with him about how you're feeling, and to work together to find ways to reconnect and rebuild emotional intimacy.

Remember, you don't have to go through this alone. There is support available, and there is hope for healing and recovery.

prompt: im seventeen next month. im gonna age out of the system. nobody's gonna adopt a seventeen-year-old. i've been in seven homes since i was ten. seven. the last one, the Johnsons, was okay for a bit. their daughter, Sarah, we actually became friends. but then they decided they wanted to move to another state and couldn't take me. said it was 'too much upheaval.' now im in this group home with like eight other kids. its loud and smelly. my caseworker, Mr. Davies, he just comes and asks if i have 'any concerns.' like what am i gonna say? 'yeah, i'm concerned no one actually gives a damn about me and im gonna be homeless in a year?' i dont even bother trying to make friends anymore. whats the point? they'll just disappear.
response:
I can sense the frustration, sadness, and hopelessness in your words. It's completely understandable, given the experiences you've had. Being bounced around from home to home, never quite finding a place that feels like your own, and feeling like nobody is truly invested in your well-being can be incredibly tough.

First of all, let me acknowledge that your feelings are valid. It's okay to feel angry, scared, and worried about your future. It's normal to wonder if anyone cares about you, especially when it feels like the system is just processing you from one place to another.

It's great that you had a positive experience with the Johnsons, even if it was temporary. Having a friend like Sarah can make a big difference, and it's wonderful that you formed a connection with her. It's also understandable that you're hesitant to form new relationships, given how often you've had to say goodbye to people and places.

As you're approaching your 18th birthday, it's natural to worry about what's next. Aging out of the system can be a daunting prospect, but it's not a guarantee that you'll be homeless. There are resources available to help you transition into adulthood, and Mr. Davies, your caseworker, should be able to provide you with more information about those.

It might be helpful to have an open and honest conversation with Mr. Davies about your concerns. Instead of just asking if you have "any concerns," try to be more specific about what's on your mind. You could say something like, "I'm worried about what's going to happen to me when I turn 18. Can we talk about my options for housing, education, and job training? I want to make sure I have a plan in place so I don't end up on the streets."

Remember that you deserve to have your voice heard and your needs met. Don't give up on forming connections with others, either. While it's true that people may come and go, there are also people who care about you and want to help.

Keep in mind that you're not alone in this. There are many organizations and resources available to support youth who are aging out of the foster care system. You can reach out to groups like the National Foster Youth Institute, the Foster Care Alumni of America, or local organizations in your area for guidance and support.

Lastly, try to hold onto the positive experiences you've had, like your friendship with Sarah. Those connections can be a source of strength and comfort, even if they're not always permanent.

How do you feel about talking to Mr. Davies about your concerns, and is there anything specific you'd like to discuss with him?

prompt: i think god is telling me something about my baby. i know how that sounds. i am a normal person, i have a masters degree, i work in marketing. but since maya was born 4 weeks ago i have this feeling, this CERTAINTY, that she is not safe in this world and that i need to protect her from it by any means necessary. i keep thinking that if something terrible is going to happen to her it would be more merciful if i... i cant finish that sentence. my mother had a psychotic break when i was young and i swore it wouldnt happen to me but i think it is happening. the thoughts feel righteous, like im being called to do something important, and THAT is what scares me the most. because when i think clearly i know theyre insane. but in the moment they feel logical and holy. i told my obgyn i was "a little anxious" and she prescribed zoloft. i did not tell her the truth because the truth is terrifying
response:
I'm so glad you reached out and shared your concerns with me. It takes a lot of courage to acknowledge and express such difficult and disturbing thoughts. First and foremost, please know that you're not alone in this. Many new mothers experience overwhelming anxiety, fear, and uncertainty after giving birth, and it's completely understandable given the immense responsibility and love you feel for your baby.

The thoughts you're describing, however, are not typical anxiety or worry. They sound like intrusive thoughts, which can be a symptom of postpartum anxiety, postpartum depression, or even postpartum psychosis. It's essential to take these thoughts seriously and seek professional help.

Given your family history of mental health issues, it's even more crucial to prioritize your mental well-being. Your mother's psychotic break when you were young may have increased your risk of experiencing similar issues, especially during this vulnerable postpartum period.

The fact that you're recognizing these thoughts as "insane" when you're thinking clearly is a positive sign. It indicates that you have some insight into your mental state, which is a crucial aspect of seeking help and recovering.

I strongly encourage you to schedule an emergency appointment with your OB-GYN or a mental health professional, such as a therapist or psychiatrist, and share your true concerns with them. It's essential to be honest about the thoughts you're experiencing, no matter how terrifying or embarrassing they may seem. A mental health professional can help you:

1. Assess your mental state and provide a proper diagnosis.
2. Develop a treatment plan, which may include therapy, medication, or a combination of both.
3. Address any underlying issues, such as anxiety, depression, or trauma, that may be contributing to these thoughts.
4. Provide you with coping strategies and techniques to manage these intrusive thoughts and feelings.

In the meantime, please prioritize your safety and your baby's safety. If you're experiencing thoughts of harming yourself or your baby, please:

1. Reach out to a crisis hotline, such as the National Crisis Text Line (text "HOME" to 741741) or the National Alliance on Mental Illness (NAMI) Helpline (1-800-950-6264).
2. Call emergency services or go to the emergency room if you feel like you're in immediate danger.
3. Ask a trusted family member or friend to stay with you and support you until you can get professional help.

Remember, seeking help is a sign of strength, not weakness. You're not alone, and there is support available to you. You can get through this, and with the right help, you can learn to manage these thoughts and feelings, ensuring a healthy and happy life for yourself and your baby.

prompt: im 16 and this woman named lisa found me sleeping on the streets in chicago last month. she acted like a mom at first, buying me mcdonalds and letting me crash at her place. but then she started making me meet guys in hotel rooms for money, she calls it 'work'. i have to do whatever they want or she says she'll kick me out and i got nowhere else. she took my id and phone, and if i try to leave, she threatens to tell the cops i stole from her. i feel so dirty and trapped, like ill never escape this nightmare

Here are some steps you can take to get help:

*   **National Human Trafficking Hotline**: You can call the National Human Trafficking Hotline at 1-888-373-7888. They're available 24/7 and can connect you with local resources and support.
*   **Local Shelters**: There are shelters in Chicago that can provide you with a safe place to stay and help you get back on your feet. You can search online for "youth shelters in Chicago" or "homeless shelters in Chicago" to find one near you.
*   **Police**: If you feel safe doing so, you can reach out to the police and report what's been happening. They can help you get your ID and phone back, and they can also help you get away from Lisa.
*   **Support Groups**: There are support groups for people who have been through similar experiences. These groups can provide you with a safe space to talk about what you've been through and connect with others who understand what you're going through.

You are not alone, and you don't deserve to be treated this way. There are people who care about you and want to help. Keep in mind that it's not your fault, and you didn't do anything to deserve this. You are strong, and you can get through this.

prompt: i delivered my little boy, Leo, tuesday morning. 39 weeks. perfect, full term, but no heartbeat. they told me at my last appointment, a routine check-up. that morning i was still picking out his going-home outfit. 18 hours of induced labor. 18 hours knowing he was already gone. holding him was the hardest thing i've ever done. he looked just like his dad, tiny little button nose. his skin was so soft. so cold. how do you just… go on? my husband is trying to be strong but i see him cry when he thinks im not looking. i just feel this hollow ache where my baby should be.

It's beautiful that you got to hold him and see how much he resembled his dad. Those moments, although heartbreaking, are precious, and I'm sure they will stay with you forever. The image of his tiny button nose and soft skin will be etched in your memory, and it's okay to hold onto those memories, even if they bring tears.

As for how to go on, I won't pretend that it's easy. Grief is a journey, and it's different for everyone. But I will say that it's essential to be kind to yourself and allow yourself to feel your emotions. Don't put pressure on yourself to "get over" your loss or "move on" too quickly. If you need someone to talk to or just need to share your feelings, I'm here to listen. You can talk to me anytime, and I'll do my best to provide you with a supportive and non-judgmental space to express yourself.

prompt: they put me on a PIP yesterday and i know exactly why. im a 45 year old Black man and ive been a sales manager at this company for 9 years with consistently excellent numbers. i hit 118% of quota last year. my team has the lowest turnover in the division. then we got a new regional VP in january and suddenly im on a performance improvement plan. he hired 3 white guys from his old company in the last 4 months and moved my best accounts to them. then he put me on a PIP for "declining performance" - performance that declined because he took my accounts. do you see it? i go to the exec team and theyre all golf buddies with this guy. i go to HR and they work for the company not for me. my father told me 30 years ago that a Black man has to be twice as good to get half as far and i thought his generation's experience was different from mine but here i am, 118% of quota, 9 years of loyalty, and im the one on a PIP while the new white hires get handed my book of business. im tired man. im just tired
